common.brandTagline: Messages & files in sync across devices

metadata.title: ShrimpSend | Reliable File Transfer Across Any Network
metadata.description: Send files between your devices — even on difficult networks. Browser-based receiving without asking others to install anything, resume large transfers after disconnects, and reliable delivery across hotel Wi-Fi, campus networks, and carrier NAT.
metadata.registerDescription: Create your account and verify email to sync everywhere

cookieConsent.title: Cookies and privacy notice
cookieConsent.body: We use necessary cookies and local storage for sign-in, language, theme, device identity, and transfer preferences. Anonymous analytics only runs if you allow it.
cookieConsent.necessaryDesc: Keeps sign-in, language, theme, device identity, and transfer preferences working. This cannot be disabled.
cookieConsent.analyticsDesc: Helps us understand page visits and product usage. It is not used for cross-site ad tracking.

errors.s3NotConfigured: S3 is not configured. Please configure it in Settings first.

corsLikely.titleUpload: Upload failed: network issue or missing CORS
corsLikely.titleDownload: Download failed: network issue or missing CORS
corsLikely.body: The browser blocked the request. Common causes are network interruptions or the target (your bucket / LAN device) not allowing this page's origin in its CORS rules. If your network looks fine, please add the origin below to the target's CORS AllowedOrigins.
corsLikely.originLabel: Current page origin (copy this to your CORS AllowedOrigins)

landing.headline: Transfer files between devices — even on difficult networks.
landing.subhead: LAN when possible. Relay when needed. Resume when interrupted.
landing.heroSubhead: LAN when possible. Relay when needed. Resume when interrupted. ShrimpSend keeps your own devices connected when ordinary tools give up on unstable or restrictive networks.
landing.heroMessageDesc: Network dropped at 68%. Pick up the transfer from where it stopped instead of starting over from 0%.
landing.heroFootnote: Available for macOS, Windows, Android, iOS, and Web.
landing.showcaseAlt: ShrimpSend on Mac, Web, and mobile — send and receive files across your devices
landing.featuresTitle: Reliable transfer, still built for maximum speed
landing.featureNoInstallDesc: Send files directly to browsers and temporary devices. Perfect when the other side cannot install software — client machines, guest devices, or one-off shares.
landing.featureResumeDesc: Large transfers survive unstable networks. Reconnect and continue from the interrupted position instead of restarting from 0%. Native client ↔ client today; client ↔ web not yet.
landing.featureRestrictiveNetworkDesc: Transfers still work behind NAT, campus Wi-Fi, and carrier networks — even when devices cannot directly reach each other.
landing.featureMultiThreadDesc: Parallel chunks per job for higher throughput.
landing.featureMultiParallelDesc: Many devices transferring at once without blocking each other.
landing.featureOfflineMdnsDesc: Native apps only: no sign-in; LAN discovery via mDNS — traffic stays local. Web does not support local discovery.
landing.featureBreakOneWayDesc: Available after sign-in only: server-assisted path detection punches through one-way firewall/NAT constraints.
landing.featureLanWebDesc: On the same LAN: browser-to-browser and app-to-web transfer directly without hitting the public internet.
landing.featureWebrtcDesc: Optional WebRTC data path on the local network.
landing.featureS3WanDesc: When LAN direct transfer is unavailable or cross-network delivery fails, S3 acts as the fallback path so files can still be delivered reliably.
landing.compareDesc: Go as fast as your LAN allows on direct paths. When networks turn unstable or restrictive, stay reliable without losing progress or starting over.
landing.compareScenario1ShrimpSend: Reconnect → resume from where it stopped
landing.compareScenario2Title: Send to a device you cannot install on
landing.compareScenario2Others: Both sides need the same app installed
landing.compareScenario2ShrimpSend: Open a browser and receive — no install needed
landing.compareScenario3Others: Direct connection fails; LAN-only tools stop working
landing.compareScenario3ShrimpSend: Automatic relay keeps the transfer path alive
landing.stepOneDesc: Choose your phone, desktop, or a browser endpoint for temporary receiving — even when the other side cannot install anything.
landing.stepTwoDesc: Prefer LAN on the same network, use relay across restrictive networks, and fall back to S3 when you need a stable wide-area path for large files.
landing.stepThreeDesc: Transfers finish on difficult networks. If something drops, native clients resume from the interrupted position instead of forcing you to start over.
landing.docsTitle: Tune LAN transfer and S3 for your network
landing.docsDesc: Read about S3-compatible storage, CORS rules, and provider notes for wide-area large files, private object storage, and team deployments.
landing.pricingTitle: Start free. Upgrade when you need more.
landing.pricingDesc: Pricing follows the current mainland or overseas cluster automatically and is visible without signing in. When you choose to buy, signed-out users go to sign-in first and return to the purchase page automatically.
landing.pricingOverseasFreeLine: 1GB hosted upload per UTC calendar month
landing.pricingDomesticMiniLine: One-time purchase for lightweight personal use
landing.pricingDomesticProLine: One-time purchase for more long-term devices
landing.faqOfflineQ: Does the recipient need to install an app?
landing.faqOfflineA: Not always. Recipients can receive in a browser without installing ShrimpSend — ideal for guest devices, restricted work machines, or one-off shares. Native apps unlock LAN discovery, resume, and server-assisted paths across restrictive networks.
landing.faqLanLoginQ: Does it work on hotel Wi-Fi, campus networks, or carrier NAT?
landing.faqLanLoginA: Yes — that is a core use case. When direct device-to-device paths are blocked, ShrimpSend can use server-assisted relay to keep transfers working. Native apps can still discover each other on the same LAN without signing in; signing in unlocks cross-network relay and web participation in your device session.
landing.faqWebQ: What happens if my network drops during a large transfer?
landing.faqWebA: On native clients, large transfers can resume from the interrupted position after reconnecting — no need to restart from 0%. Client ↔ web resume is not supported yet; use native endpoints on both sides for largest files on unstable networks.
landing.faqS3A: Use S3-compatible storage when you often send large files across cities or networks, or want file staging in your own object storage.
landing.downloadsHint: Open 123pan or Google Drive for your region to download installers for each platform.

devApiEnv.title: Debug API environment
devApiEnv.description: Shown only on LAN-style hosts while running the Next.js dev server. Local debugging is pinned to local service ports.
devApiEnv.hint: Local Web always uses the current host's :9000 backend and :8000 WebSocket, and no longer connects directly to production APIs. To test overseas membership, Stripe, or hosted storage, run the local backend with the dev-overseas profile.

errors.deviceUnavailable: Selected devices are unavailable; choose again
errors.httpStall: HTTP transfer stalled or timed out — check the receiver and network
errors.s3Unavailable: S3 is unavailable — check settings or test the connection
errors.webrtcTryHttp: WebRTC send failed — try HTTP mode
errors.preflightPartial: Preflight incomplete; sent anyway — restart the receiver if it still fails
errors.webrtcNotSupported: WebRTC is not available — switch transport
errors.webrtcRetryHttp: WebRTC retry failed — send via HTTP

errors.webrtcRetryS3: WebRTC retry failed — send via S3

connectionDiag.title: Connection diagnostic
connectionDiag.reasonHttpDirectFail: Cannot reach peer HTTP service (timeout or no response)
connectionDiag.reasonHttpSignalingFail: Signaling probe failed — peer HTTP did not respond
connectionDiag.reasonHttpPullOk: Peer can pull files from this device
connectionDiag.reasonHttpPullFail: Reverse pull failed — peer cannot reach this device's HTTP
connectionDiag.reasonWebrtcOnline: Same network — WebRTC can connect directly
connectionDiag.reasonHttpDirectNoUrl: No LAN address found — cannot test HTTP direct
connectionDiag.reasonOfflineCloud: Offline — cannot run cloud signaling probes
connectionDiag.reasonS3LoginRequired: Sign in required to test S3
connectionDiag.helpHttpDirectBody:
This device sends a /probe request directly to the peer's LAN HTTP address without going through the cloud.

Confirms whether the fastest HTTP file path works when a LAN address is known (e.g. via mDNS) and the network is reachable.
connectionDiag.helpHttpSignalingBody:
Via cloud messages (Centrifugo), asks the peer to self-check its HTTP service and report back.

Confirms peer HTTP health when both sides are online, even before a LAN address is known, and may discover or refresh the LAN URL.
connectionDiag.helpHttpPullBody:
Via cloud, asks the peer to reach this device's HTTP service.

Confirms whether the peer can pull files when NAT or asymmetric routing blocks direct HTTP — a fallback when direct LAN HTTP fails.
connectionDiag.helpWebrtcBody:
Exchanges ICE candidates via cloud to see if both sides share a network, can P2P directly, or need relay.

Confirms whether WebRTC file transfer is viable (often slower than HTTP direct but works across networks).
connectionDiag.helpS3Body:
Checks that S3 storage is configured and runs a cloud connectivity test.

Confirms whether S3 relay is available when all LAN/direct paths fail.

overview.title: ShrimpSend docs: device conversations for messages and files
overview.lead: ShrimpSend is not a traditional share-link tool. It organizes phones, desktops, and browsers into device conversations so text, clipboard snippets, media, and large files arrive at the device you choose.
overview.whatTitle: A transfer tool designed around your devices
overview.whatBody: Think of ShrimpSend as a cross-device conversation: choose a target device, send messages or files, and the app picks the right path from LAN direct transfer, WebRTC, S3 fallback, and more. Native apps can discover and transfer on LAN without signing in; signing in lets the web app join and helps with more complex one-way network cases.
overview.stepInstallTitle: Install or open the web app
overview.stepInstallBody: Open ShrimpSend on mobile, desktop, or browser. Native apps are best for LAN discovery and daily use; the web app is convenient for temporary send and receive.
overview.stepDevicesBody: After signing in, you can see your device list. Native clients on the same LAN can also discover and transfer directly.
overview.stepSendBody: Drop text, clipboard snippets, images, videos, or large files into the conversation and ShrimpSend chooses a suitable transfer path.
overview.featuresBody: Beyond the existing S3 setup guide, the docs should explain device conversations, LAN direct transfer, web participation, cross-network fallback, security boundaries, and membership pricing.
overview.featureDevicesBody: The workflow is centered on devices, not share links. Content lands directly in the selected device conversation.
overview.featureMdnsBody: Native clients can use mDNS discovery and LAN transfer for office, home, and same-Wi-Fi scenarios.
overview.featureS3Body: Across networks, with large files, or when LAN paths are unavailable, use hosted or custom S3 for stable relay.
overview.transferBody: ShrimpSend prefers the most direct path first. On LAN it tries direct transfer; the web app can participate through browser capabilities. When the network is constrained or cross-network, it falls back to S3.
overview.localBody: Best when devices are on the same network. Native clients can transfer on LAN without signing in; after signing in, the web app can join the same device conversation and transfer on LAN too.
overview.wideBody: Best for cross-city, cross-network, and large-file scenarios. Use hosted quota or configure your own S3-compatible object storage.
overview.securityBody: LAN direct transfer does not mean traffic must pass through our servers. Signing in is mainly for device identity, web sessions, and server-assisted discovery in difficult networks. For custom S3, use scoped credentials and configure bucket CORS correctly.
overview.s3Body: The S3 guide already covers Endpoint, Region, Bucket, credentials, CORS, common providers, and deployment links. Future docs can expand into LAN transfer, WebRTC, device management, and membership pages.
overview.faqLoginQ: Why does LAN transfer sometimes ask me to sign in?
overview.faqLoginA: Native clients can transfer on the same LAN without signing in. Signing in lets the web app join the device conversation and enables server-assisted discovery for one-way firewall/NAT cases.
overview.faqWebQ: Can the web app transfer directly on LAN?
overview.faqWebA: Yes. The web app is limited by browser capabilities, but after signing in it can join the same device conversation and transfer directly on the same LAN.
overview.faqS3A: Use S3 when devices are not on the same network, large files need a stable relay, or you want data staged in your own object storage.

s3.lead: The web app uploads to presigned URLs from the browser. Besides endpoint, credentials, and IAM/bucket policies, you must configure bucket CORS so it matches the exact origin you use to open the app. Missing or mismatched CORS causes failed uploads or multipart completion issues.
s3.fieldsBody:
Endpoint: S3 API base URL including the scheme (usually https://).
Region: Must match the bucket region / vendor naming.
Bucket: Bucket used for relay/temporary storage.
Access Key ID / Secret Access Key: Prefer a scoped sub-user with PutObject, GetObject, and multipart upload permissions (including list/complete as required).
s3.corsWhy: The browser sends cross-origin PUT/POST to your bucket and preflights with x-amz-* headers. Your bucket CORS must allow the origin of the page where users open the web app; if that exact URL is missing, the browser blocks the upload.
s3.corsExamplesBody: Allow the exact site root where users open the web app: scheme + host (+ port), no path, no trailing slash. This page reads your current tab and shows that origin below—copy it into your bucket CORS AllowedOrigins as-is.
s3.corsCurrentOriginLabel: Copy this origin (matches your address bar prefix)
s3.corsCurrentOriginFoot: If users also reach the app via other URLs (different http/https, www vs apex, another host/port), open each of those URLs in the browser, open this guide again, and add every origin shown—missing one breaks uploads for that entry point.
s3.corsOrigins: Must match the string in the box above and the failing request’s Origin header. Use the web app origin, not your S3 endpoint hostname.
s3.corsMethods: AllowedMethods: Include at least GET, PUT, POST, DELETE, HEAD (multipart uses PUT, etc.).
s3.corsHeaders: AllowedHeaders: Prefer * or at least content-type, content-md5, x-amz-*, x-amz-date, authorization as required by your vendor.
s3.corsExpose: ExposeHeaders: Include ETag. Multipart completion often needs to read ETag from the response; omitting it can break the flow.
s3.corsMaxAge: MaxAgeSeconds: e.g. 86400 to reduce OPTIONS traffic.
s3.aws: AWS S3: Bucket → Permissions → CORS. Endpoint is usually the regional virtual-hosted style URL.
s3.cos: Tencent COS: Bucket → Security → CORS. Endpoint like https://cos.{region}.myqcloud.com.
s3.oss: Alibaba OSS: Bucket → Access control → CORS. Use the console endpoint (public/internal) as shown.
s3.r2: Cloudflare R2: Bucket Settings → CORS policy. Use the S3 API endpoint from the R2 dashboard.
s3.rustfs: RustFS: set RUSTFS_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS in Compose for ShrimpSend web Origins; use the S3 API URL as Endpoint with Path-style on.
s3.opsBody: Mobile clients receive the documentation URL from the backend for the active deployment. If it opens the wrong host, set APP_PUBLIC_WEB_BASE_URL on the server to your real web root (no trailing slash).
